commit 103d299150f2c7a385fca0b7e0f840253c5c5c00
Author: Mikhail Gavrilov <mikhail.v.gavrilov@gmail.com>

dma-debug: suppress cacheline overlap warning when arch has no DMA alignment requirement

This commit suppresses the DMA cacheline overlap warning when the architecture
does not require cacheline-aligned DMA buffers. The change aims to reduce
false positives from small allocations on coherent architectures like x86.

Link: https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=215740

> diff --git a/kernel/dma/debug.c b/kernel/dma/debug.c
> index 0677918f06a8..1a725edbbbf6 100644
> --- a/kernel/dma/debug.c
> +++ b/kernel/dma/debug.c
> @@ -615,6 +615,7 @@ static void add_dma_entry(struct dma_debug_entry *entry, unsigned long attrs)
>  	} else if (rc == -EEXIST &&
>  		   !(attrs & DMA_ATTR_SKIP_CPU_SYNC) &&
>  		   !(entry->is_cache_clean && overlap_cache_clean) &&
> +		   dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES &&

Does this change defeat the primary purpose of the DMA debug infrastructure?

CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG is intended to act as a cross-platform portability
checker, allowing developers to test drivers on forgiving, cache-coherent
architectures like x86 and catch violations that would cause memory corruption
on non-coherent architectures.

By conditioning this warning on dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES,
the check is effectively disabled on x86, where dma_get_cache_alignment()
defaults to 1.

While this suppresses false positives from independent small kmalloc()
allocations sharing a cacheline, won't it also completely hide genuine
portability bugs, such as a driver mapping two adjacent fields of the same
structure?

>  		   !(IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC) &&
>  		     is_swiotlb_active(entry->dev))) {
>  		err_printk(entry->dev, entry,

Target Commit:
commit 103d299150f2c7a385fca0b7e0f840253c5c5c00
Author: Mikhail Gavrilov <mikhail.v.gavrilov@gmail.com>
Date:   Fri Mar 27 12:41:56 2026 +0000

    dma-debug: suppress cacheline overlap warning when arch has no DMA alignment requirement
    
    When CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG is enabled, the DMA debug infrastructure
    tracks active mappings per cacheline and warns if two different DMA
    mappings share the same cacheline ("cacheline tracking EEXIST,
    overlapping mappings aren't supported").
    
    On x86_64, ARCH_KMALLOC_MINALIGN defaults to 8, so small kmalloc
    allocations (e.g. the 8-byte hub->buffer and hub->status in the USB
    hub driver) frequently land in the same 64-byte cacheline.  When both
    are DMA-mapped, this triggers a false positive warning.
    
    This has been reported repeatedly since v5.14 (when the EEXIST check
    was added) across various USB host controllers and devices including
    xhci_hcd with USB hubs, USB audio devices, and USB ethernet adapters.
    
    The cacheline overlap is only a real concern on architectures that
    require DMA buffer alignment to cacheline boundaries (i.e. where
    ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN >= L1_CACHE_BYTES).  On architectures like x86_64
    where dma_get_cache_alignment() returns 1, the hardware is
    cache-coherent and overlapping cacheline mappings are harmless.
    
    Suppress the EEXIST warning when dma_get_cache_alignment() is less
    than L1_CACHE_BYTES, indicating the architecture does not require
    cacheline-aligned DMA buffers.
    
    Verified with a kernel module reproducer that performs two kmalloc(8)
    allocations back-to-back and DMA-maps both:
    
      Before: allocations share a cacheline, EEXIST fires within ~50 pairs
      After:  same cacheline pair found, but no warning emitted
    
    Fixes: 2b4bbc6231d7 ("dma-debug: report -EEXIST errors in add_dma_entry")
    Link: https://bugzilla.kernel.org/show_bug.cgi?id=215740
    Suggested-by: Harry Yoo <harry@kernel.org>
    Tested-by: Mikhail Gavrilov <mikhail.v.gavrilov@gmail.com>
    Signed-off-by: Mikhail Gavrilov <mikhail.v.gavrilov@gmail.com>

diff --git a/kernel/dma/debug.c b/kernel/dma/debug.c
index 0677918f06a8..1a725edbbbf6 100644
--- a/kernel/dma/debug.c
+++ b/kernel/dma/debug.c
@@ -615,6 +615,7 @@ static void add_dma_entry(struct dma_debug_entry *entry, unsigned long attrs)
 	} else if (rc == -EEXIST &&
 		   !(attrs & DMA_ATTR_SKIP_CPU_SYNC) &&
 		   !(entry->is_cache_clean && overlap_cache_clean) &&
+		   dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES &&
 		   !(IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC) &&
 		     is_swiotlb_active(entry->dev))) {
 		err_printk(entry->dev, entry,

Okay, so this commit suppresses a cacheline overlap warning (`EEXIST`) within `kernel/dma/debug.c` when `dma_get_cache_alignment()` returns a value smaller than `L1_CACHE_BYTES`. The stated rationale is that this warning is irrelevant on cache-coherent architectures like x86_64, where overlapping cachelines are harmless because the hardware handles the coherence. The specific scenario mentioned is small `kmalloc` allocations (like the 8-byte buffers in a USB hub driver) that might share a 64-byte cacheline on x86_64.

The key concern arises: even on coherent architectures, isn't the cacheline overlap check valuable for finding *portability* issues? The DMA API is designed to catch bugs that could cause problems on non-coherent architectures. If we suppress this warning on x86_64, a *huge* testing platform, developers won't see these errors, and the bug will only surface on a less-frequently-tested platform like ARM32 or some ARM64 or MIPS. That's a major problem!

The commit message itself suggests this is only a concern on architectures requiring cacheline-aligned DMA buffers. The code in question is supposed to catch *portability* issues. How can we ensure that?

Let's revisit the DMA API: Can drivers legally map two *different* parts of the same cacheline for DMA? The documentation is clear: it's not allowed if the device isn't cache-coherent! Overlapping DMA mappings can cause data corruption due to cache invalidation/flushing. It is a bug if two `kmalloc` allocations end up in the same cacheline and are DMA-mapped.

Now, recall recent work on x86_64: The minimum `kmalloc` alignment was reduced (with work like Vlastimil Babka's contributions). This can lead to small allocations within the same cacheline. On x86_64 this is typically *safe* due to coherence. However, on architectures that *require* cacheline alignment, `kmalloc` would guarantee that the allocations are *aligned* to, say, 64 or 128 bytes, effectively placing the allocations in *different* cachelines. This means a driver doing `kmalloc(8)` and then DMA-mapping that memory is fine *and* portable.

Ah, but if those two allocations are instead parts of the *same* struct:
```c

    char buf1[8];
    char buf2[8];
};
```
and a driver attempts to DMA map `buf1` and `buf2`, this is a *bug* regardless of `kmalloc` alignment. This would break on an architecture that *requires* cacheline-aligned DMA mappings. This is a core part of testing on x86 for *portability* reasons.

The commit's focus on the USB hub driver's `hub->buffer` and `hub->status` suggests this might be the core issue. Are those separate `kmalloc` allocations? If so, the overlap on x86 *is* a false positive because the `kmalloc` implementation handles alignment on non-coherent architectures, where the allocations would *not* be in the same cacheline.

So, the current DMA debug tracks cachelines. It checks if active DMA mappings share the same cacheline. If `dma_get_cache_alignment() < L1_CACHE_BYTES`, the `add_dma_entry` function skips the warning.

Here's the problem: is `dma_get_cache_alignment()` the right criterion here? On x86, it's 1. Suppressing the warning based on *that* value means we *lose* the ability to catch the `struct foo` bug I mentioned! A driver with `struct foo` on x86 wouldn't get a warning, but that code would *break* on ARM. DMA debug is designed to find this *portability* issue. We're defeating that purpose!

So, the key condition is `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`. If this evaluates to false, the warning is suppressed. This *seems* correct, but what are the implications? If `dma_get_cache_alignment()` returns a value smaller than `L1_CACHE_BYTES`, it implies the architecture is either fully cache-coherent or handles coherency at a smaller granularity than the L1 cacheline. The most interesting case involves architectures that might have `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` smaller than `L1_CACHE_BYTES`, like RISC-V, where `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is `L1_CACHE_BYTES`.     "content": "spLet's look at what the commit does again.
`dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`

Wait, on some architectures, `dma_get_cache_alignment()` might return a value *less* than `L1_CACHE_BYTES` if they configure it that way.
Actually, if `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is not defined (like x86), `dma_get_cache_alignment()` returns 1. `1 >= 64` is false.
If an architecture defines `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` as 32, and `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 64. `32 >= 64` is false. Is this correct?
If `dma_get_cache_alignment() < L1_CACHE_BYTES`, it suppresses the warning.
But if `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is 32 and `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 64, it might be possible that cache lines *are* 64 bytes. If the hardware is not cache-coherent, and two 32-byte buffers share a 64-byte cacheline, then DMA invalidation could corrupt the other buffer! So it *should* warn if `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN < L1_CACHE_BYTES` but the hardware isn't coherent!
Wait, but if `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN < L1_CACHE_BYTES`, then `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is misconfigured.
Let's see: `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is usually `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` (or dynamically discovered, like on ARM64).
If `dma_get_cache_alignment() < L1_CACHE_BYTES`, what if we are on a fully coherent architecture (like x86), `dma_get_cache_alignment() == 1`.
Is there any architecture where `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`?
Yes, on ARM64 `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 64. `dma_get_cache_alignment()` dynamically returns 128 (if no CWG), or 64. So it will be >= 64.
On MIPS, `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` might be 128. `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 32. It will be >= 32.

Wait, look at `is_swiotlb_active(entry->dev)`. It says `!(IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC) && is_swiotlb_active(entry->dev))`.
Wait, on x86_64, `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is not defined. `dma_get_cache_alignment()` returns 1. `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 64.
The warning triggers when `rc == -EEXIST && dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`.
Wait, if `dma_get_cache_alignment() < L1_CACHE_BYTES`, it suppresses the warning.
What if an architecture requires cacheline alignment but its `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is somehow less than `L1_CACHE_BYTES`? Is that possible?
If `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is less than `L1_CACHE_BYTES`, it literally means DMA doesn't require alignment up to the L1 cacheline size.
Which means either the DMA is coherent (so no alignment is required at all, e.g. x86), or the architecture has a serious bug where its DMA alignment requirement is less than its L1 cache size. If it's a bug in the arch, that's not `dma/debug.c`'s fault.

Wait! Could `dma_get_cache_alignment()` return the cacheline size in bytes, and `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is the size in bytes? Yes.
So if `dma_get_cache_alignment() == 1`, it's not >= 64, so no warning.

But is there a case where `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is 1, but we STILL want to warn about overlapping mapping?
Why does `dma_debug` track cachelines? To catch false sharing that leads to memory corruption during DMA operations.
If the architecture is cache-coherent (e.g. x86), is overlapping mapping harmless?
Yes, if the DMA controllers and the CPU snoop each other's caches, there's no need to manually invalidate/flush cachelines. The cacheline tracking check was added to catch bugs on non-coherent architectures where DMA unmaps do manual invalidate, which can destroy adjacent data in the same cacheline.

    "content": "spOkay, the condition in the patch is `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`.
What happens if `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is 32 (like on some MIPS platforms) and `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 32.
Then 32 >= 32 is true. It warns.
What if `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is 32, but `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 128?
Is there any such architecture?
If `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is 32, but `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 128, then it would suppress the warning, but `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is wrong because it needs to be >= L1_CACHE_BYTES to prevent cacheline sharing.
So in any CORRECT architecture configuration, `dma_get_cache_alignment()` must be >= `L1_CACHE_BYTES` if it is not a fully coherent architecture (like x86) where it is 1.
So the condition `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES` correctly catches the "we require cacheline alignment" architectures.

Wait, is there any other check we need?
Is it possible that `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is 1 (cache coherent), BUT we *still* want to track overlapping mappings?
The commit message says: "The cacheline overlap is only a real concern on architectures that require DMA buffer alignment to cacheline boundaries ... On architectures like x86_64 where dma_get_cache_alignment() returns 1, the hardware is cache-coherent and overlapping cacheline mappings are harmless."
Is this completely true?
If two devices DMA to the exact same bytes, it's a bug. But cacheline tracking tracks overlapping mappings in the same CACHELINE, not the exact same bytes. If they map different bytes in the same cacheline, x86 is fine with that.
Wait, what if `entry->is_cache_clean` is true?
If it's true, it means `attrs & (DMA_ATTR_DEBUGGING_IGNORE_CACHELINES | DMA_ATTR_REQUIRE_COHERENT)` is set.
Wait, wait!
If `is_swiotlb_active(entry->dev)` is true, does x86 bounce the unaligned kmallocs?
If `CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC` is set and swiotlb is active, there's another condition:
`!(IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC) && is_swiotlb_active(entry->dev))`

Wait, if `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES` is added, on x86, it will ALWAYS suppress the warning because `1 >= 64` is false.
Even if SWIOTLB is bouncing unaligned kmallocs?
Wait. If SWIOTLB is bouncing unaligned kmallocs, does it bounce based on cacheline size?
On x86, does it bounce unaligned kmallocs? No, `CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC` is generally NOT enabled on x86, or if it is, it's because it needs bounce buffers for DMA addressing limits, but not for "unaligned kmalloc" since x86 is coherent.
Wait! If x86 is coherent, but a device is using bounce buffers (SWIOTLB) because it's a 32-bit DMA device on a 64-bit system with memory > 4GB...
Does SWIOTLB cause problems with overlapping cachelines on x86?
If two `kmalloc(8)` allocations are in the same cacheline, and both are DMA mapped.
SWIOTLB will allocate bounce buffers for BOTH. They will be mapped to DIFFERENT bounce buffers.
Then, on unmap, SWIOTLB copies the bounce buffers back to the original memory.
Wait!
If SWIOTLB copies back to the original memory, it uses `memcpy()` or similar!
If it uses `memcpy()` to `8` bytes, does it corrupt the adjacent `8` bytes?
No, `memcpy()` only writes the `8` bytes. It does not overwrite the adjacent `8` bytes, unlike a cacheline invalidate!
So on x86, SWIOTLB bounce buffers on unaligned kmallocs are PERFECTLY FINE because the CPU copies back the exact byte ranges, not the whole cacheline!

Wait! What if the device DMA writes to the original memory? x86 is coherent, so the device writes 8 bytes, the CPU caches are updated. The adjacent 8 bytes are not affected.
What if `CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC` is enabled?
This config was introduced for architectures where `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is large, to save memory by using a smaller `kmalloc` alignment and bouncing unaligned allocations.
If `CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC` is enabled, the previous patch already suppressed the warning for that case:
`!(IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC) && is_swiotlb_active(entry->dev))`
Because if unaligned kmalloc bouncing is enabled, SWIOTLB handles it by bouncing the unaligned buffers. The bounce buffers themselves are aligned and don't share cachelines (or rather, SWIOTLB allocates them safely), so the original buffers can share cachelines safely because they only get `memcpy`ed, not invalidated.
Wait, if `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES` is false on x86, the warning is suppressed. Is this safe?
Yes, because on x86, overlapping cachelines are perfectly safe, both for direct DMA and for SWIOTLB.

Wait, is there any bug in the patch logic?
The patch adds `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES &&`

If `dma_get_cache_alignment()` returns `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` on ARM, it will be 128 or 64. `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 64. It warns.
On x86, `dma_get_cache_alignment()` returns 1. `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 64. `1 >= 64` is false. Warning is suppressed.
Is it possible that `dma_get_cache_alignment()` evaluates multiple times or has side effects?
`dma_get_cache_alignment()` is usually an inline function returning a constant or a simple global variable read.
Is there any architecture where `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is NOT defined?
Wait! In `tools/include/linux/cache.h`, it's defined. But does `kernel/dma/debug.c` include a header that defines `L1_CACHE_BYTES` for all architectures?
Yes, `kernel/dma/debug.c` uses `L1_CACHE_SHIFT` in `to_cacheline_number`!
```c
#define CACHELINE_PER_PAGE_SHIFT (PAGE_SHIFT - L1_CACHE_SHIFT)
#define CACHELINES_PER_PAGE (1 << CACHELINE_PER_PAGE_SHIFT)

static phys_addr_t to_cacheline_number(struct dma_debug_entry *entry)
{
	return ((entry->paddr >> PAGE_SHIFT) << CACHELINE_PER_PAGE_SHIFT) +
		(offset_in_page(entry->paddr) >> L1_CACHE_SHIFT);
}
```
If `L1_CACHE_SHIFT` is used there, then `L1_CACHE_BYTES` must be available (since it's usually defined alongside `L1_CACHE_SHIFT`).
Wait, what if `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is not defined?
Let's check if `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is always defined.
`include/asm-generic/cache.h` defines:
```c

#define L1_CACHE_BYTES          (1 << L1_CACHE_SHIFT)
```
And most architectures define both `L1_CACHE_SHIFT` and `L1_CACHE_BYTES`. If they don't, compilation would fail, but `add_dma_entry` compiled fine for the patch author.

Is there any flaw in:
`dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`
If an architecture sets `dma_get_cache_alignment() == 32`, and `L1_CACHE_BYTES == 64`.
Then `32 >= 64` is false. So it will NOT warn!
But should it warn?
If `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is 32, and `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 64. The architecture is saying "DMA alignment is 32 bytes". But the CPU cache line is 64 bytes.
If the architecture is non-coherent, this means the CPU must flush/invalidate in 64-byte chunks.
If `kmalloc` only aligns to 32 bytes, then two 32-byte allocations can share a 64-byte cacheline.
When DMA invalidates one 32-byte allocation, it will invalidate the whole 64-byte cacheline, destroying the other allocation.
So if `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN < L1_CACHE_BYTES` on a non-coherent architecture, it is a catastrophic bug in the architecture definitions.

Wait. If `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is 32 and `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 64, then before the patch, `kernel/dma/debug.c` WOULD have warned. With the patch, it WILL NOT warn.
Should we be worried about this?

    "content": "spOkay, s390 does not define `ARCH_HAS_DMA_MINALIGN`.
So on s390, `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is 1.
Is s390 cache-coherent? Yes, s390 is famous for being cache-coherent.
So suppressing the warning on s390 is also correct.

What about `dma_get_cache_alignment()` itself?
The function takes `void` and returns an `int`.
Is there any chance `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is not a compile-time constant on some architectures?
For example, if `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is defined as `cache_line_size()`, it would be evaluated at runtime.
But usually `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is defined as `(1 << L1_CACHE_SHIFT)` which is constant.

Is there any bug like `dma_get_cache_alignment()` returning something in bytes, but `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is something else? No, both are in bytes.

Let's review the code in `kernel/dma/debug.c` where the patch modifies:
```c
	rc = active_cacheline_insert(entry, &overlap_cache_clean);
	if (rc == -ENOMEM) {
		pr_err_once("cacheline tracking ENOMEM, dma-debug disabled\n");
		global_disable = true;
	} else if (rc == -EEXIST &&
		   !(attrs & DMA_ATTR_SKIP_CPU_SYNC) &&
		   !(entry->is_cache_clean && overlap_cache_clean) &&
		   dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES &&
		   !(IS_ENABLED(CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC) &&
		     is_swiotlb_active(entry->dev))) {
		err_printk(entry->dev, entry,
			"cacheline tracking EEXIST, overlapping mappings aren't supported\n");
	}
```

Wait! `active_cacheline_insert()` still executes!
It allocates memory or inserts into the radix tree.
It still tracks the cachelines even if `dma_get_cache_alignment() < L1_CACHE_BYTES`.
Is this a memory leak or unnecessary overhead?
If we are on x86, we never care about overlapping cachelines, right?
Then why are we doing `active_cacheline_insert()`?
Because `CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG` is a debugging option. It tracks cachelines.
But the warning is ONLY omitted when it's safe.
If it tracks them, it inserts them, and then `active_cacheline_remove()` removes them.
Wait, if `active_cacheline_insert()` returns `-EEXIST`, it increments the overlap count.
If the warning is suppressed, the radix tree still correctly tracks the overlap count!
When they are removed, `active_cacheline_remove()` is called, decrementing the overlap count.
So the tracking logic is perfectly intact. Only the warning is suppressed. This is safe and avoids any radix tree corruption issues.

Wait! What if we could just NOT insert them if `dma_get_cache_alignment() < L1_CACHE_BYTES`?
If we don't insert them, we save the overhead of tracking cachelines on x86.
But `CONFIG_DMA_API_DEBUG` is a debug feature anyway, people enable it specifically to debug, so overhead is expected. Changing the core tracking logic might be riskier than just suppressing the false-positive warning.
So the current patch is the minimal, safe way to fix the false positive on x86 and other coherent architectures.

Wait, is there any problem with the condition?
`dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`
If an architecture sets `dma_get_cache_alignment() == 32`, and `L1_CACHE_BYTES == 64`.
Will it warn? No.
Does such an architecture exist?
Look at `arch/parisc/include/asm/cache.h`:
```c
#define ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN   32 // for some
#define L1_CACHE_BYTES      16
```
Here, 32 >= 16 is true, it warns.
What about `arch/mips/include/asm/mach-ip32/kmalloc.h`:
```c
#define ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN   32 // or 128
```
MIPS `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is usually 32. 32 >= 32 is true.
What about `arch/loongarch/include/asm/cache.h`:
```c
#define ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN   (16)
```
LoongArch `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is `1 << CONFIG_L1_CACHE_SHIFT`. Usually 64.
Wait, if LoongArch has `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` as 16, and `L1_CACHE_BYTES` as 64, then `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is 16. `16 >= 64` is false!
Let's check LoongArch `L1_CACHE_SHIFT`.
In `arch/loongarch/Kconfig`:
```c
config L1_CACHE_SHIFT
	int
	default "6"
```
So `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 64.
`ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is 16.
So `dma_get_cache_alignment()` returns 16.
Is 16 >= 64? No.
So on LoongArch, it will NOT warn!
But wait, is LoongArch cache-coherent?
LoongArch DMA is generally cache-coherent. The DMA doesn't require manual flush/invalidate, so overlapping is fine. `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` is only 16 to avoid too small kmallocs sharing cachelines in some cases, or maybe it's just the baseline.
Since it's coherent, suppressing the warning is correct!
Wait! Is there any architecture that is NON-coherent, but `dma_get_cache_alignment() < L1_CACHE_BYTES`?
If an architecture is NON-coherent, it MUST HAVE `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`! Because if it is smaller, then `kmalloc` will return allocations that are not aligned to the cacheline, meaning they will share cachelines. Since it's non-coherent, manual DMA flush/invalidate will corrupt the shared cachelines. The kernel would be fundamentally broken if a non-coherent arch had `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN < L1_CACHE_BYTES`.
So IF there's any non-coherent architecture, it GUARANTEES `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`. Thus, `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES` is true, and the warning WILL fire.
If it is coherent, it might have `dma_get_cache_alignment() < L1_CACHE_BYTES` (like x86 where it's 1, or LoongArch where it's 16), and the warning is suppressed, which is CORRECT because overlapping is harmless.

Are there ANY non-coherent architectures with `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN < L1_CACHE_BYTES`?
If there are, their `kmalloc` is broken anyway. They will have silent data corruption long before they hit this warning. So it's not a concern for this patch.

`add_dma_entry` has:

```c
	bool overlap_cache_clean;
	struct hash_bucket *bucket;
	unsigned long flags;

	bucket = get_hash_bucket(entry, &flags);
	hash_bucket_add(bucket, entry);
	put_hash_bucket(bucket, flags);

	rc = active_cacheline_insert(entry, &overlap_cache_clean);
```
`active_cacheline_insert` initializes `overlap_cache_clean` to `false` at the beginning:
```c
static int active_cacheline_insert(struct dma_debug_entry *entry,
				   bool *overlap_cache_clean)
{
	phys_addr_t cln = to_cacheline_number(entry);
	unsigned long flags;
	int rc;

	*overlap_cache_clean = false;
```
If `rc == -EEXIST`, it means it found an overlap, and might set `overlap_cache_clean`.
If `rc == -ENOMEM`, it handles it.
If `rc == 0`, everything is fine.

Are there any locking issues?
`active_cacheline_insert` does:
```c
	spin_lock_irqsave(&radix_lock, flags);
	rc = radix_tree_insert(&dma_active_cacheline, cln, entry);
	if (rc == -EEXIST) {
		struct dma_debug_entry *existing;

		active_cacheline_inc_overlap(cln, entry->is_cache_clean);
		existing = radix_tree_lookup(&dma_active_cacheline, cln);
		/* A lookup failure here after we got -EEXIST is unexpected. */
		if (existing)
			*overlap_cache_clean = existing->is_cache_clean;
	}
	spin_unlock_irqrestore(&radix_lock, flags);
	return rc;
```
This is fully internal to `active_cacheline_insert` and correctly restores the lock.

What if `entry` is inserted, then `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES` prevents the warning.
What if we DO NOT warn, but the overlap count reaches the maximum?
In `active_cacheline_inc_overlap`:
```c
static void active_cacheline_inc_overlap(phys_addr_t cln, bool cache_clean)
{
	int overlap = active_cacheline_read_overlap(cln);

	overlap = active_cacheline_set_overlap(cln, ++overlap);

	/* If we overflowed the overlap counter then we're potentially going
	 * to leak dma-mappings.
	 */
	WARN_ONCE(overlap > ACTIVE_CACHELINE_MAX_OVERLAP,
		  "DMA-API: exceeded %d overlapping mappings of cacheline %pa\n",
		  ACTIVE_CACHELINE_MAX_OVERLAP, &cln);
}
```
So it will still WARN_ONCE if we get too many overlapping mappings of the exact same cacheline (e.g., 63 mappings, since MAX_OVERLAP is `(1<<6)-1` = 63).
So if we have 64 small kmalloc(8) objects in the same 64-byte cacheline, and all are DMA mapped, `overlap` will be up to 8. So it won't overflow the counter.
Wait, `kmalloc(8)` in a 64-byte cacheline only has 8 slots. 8 mappings. So it won't hit the 63 maximum overlap.
So the tracking works perfectly, doesn't overflow, just suppresses the EEXIST warning for overlapping mapping!

`dma_get_cache_alignment` returns `int`. `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is `(1 << L1_CACHE_SHIFT)`.
Since `L1_CACHE_SHIFT` is typically 5 or 6, `(1 << 6)` is 64, which is an `int`.

The commit message asserts:
"On architectures like x86_64 where dma_get_cache_alignment() returns 1, the hardware is cache-coherent and overlapping cacheline mappings are harmless."
Is it ALWAYS harmless?
If two devices DMA to the same cacheline on x86:
Device A DMAs 8 bytes into the first 8 bytes.
Device B DMAs 8 bytes into the second 8 bytes.
x86 handles cache coherence through hardware snooping. The CPU cache line will be invalidated/updated by the memory controller snoops at the byte or cacheline level without software intervention. As long as the two devices don't write to the EXACT SAME bytes, it's perfectly safe.
What if a device does a DMA read and another does a DMA write?
Still safe, hardware snoops maintain coherence.
Software cacheline tracking is ONLY needed for architectures that require manual software cache maintenance (flush/invalidate), which operates at cacheline granularity and can destroy adjacent data in the same cacheline.
Since x86 doesn't do software cache maintenance (except for non-coherent mappings like `dma_sync_*` with `DMA_ATTR_NON_COHERENT` which is highly specific and not the default `kmalloc` case), the cacheline tracking for overlapping mapping is completely unnecessary for correctness on x86.

Wait, what if a device on x86 IS non-coherent?
There are some extremely rare devices on x86 that are non-coherent (e.g. some obscure integrated GPUs or similar?). But x86 DMA API implementation usually doesn't do cache maintenance even for them, or it does it through other means. Actually, x86 `dma_sync_single_for_cpu` is a no-op (except for SWIOTLB bounce). The hardware is coherent for all standard DMA.
If `dma_sync_single_for_cpu` is a no-op, then there is NO software cache maintenance. Therefore, no cacheline corruption.
Therefore, overlapping cachelines are 100% harmless on x86.

What about ARM64?
ARM64 is mostly coherent but supports non-coherent devices.
Its `dma_get_cache_alignment()` returns 64 or 128. `L1_CACHE_BYTES` is 64.
Since `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES` is true, the warning is STILL EMITTED on ARM64!
This is correct because ARM64 has non-coherent devices and requires cacheline alignment.

What about ARM32?
`dma_get_cache_alignment()` = `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` = `L1_CACHE_BYTES`.
Condition is `L1_CACHE_BYTES >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`, which is true. Warning emitted.

What about RISC-V?
`dma_get_cache_alignment()` returns `ARCH_DMA_MINALIGN` (which is `L1_CACHE_BYTES`).
Condition is true. Warning emitted.

Because `&&` short-circuits, if `CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC` is enabled, `is_swiotlb_active(entry->dev)` is evaluated.
With the patch, it is evaluated ONLY IF `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES` is true.
So the patch actually REDUCES the chance of a NULL dereference! (On x86, it prevents it).
So if anything, it's safer.

Are there any logic inversions?
`dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES` means "architecture requires cacheline alignment (is non-coherent or uses strict alignment)".

Wait, `active_cacheline_insert` does:

```c
		active_cacheline_inc_overlap(cln, entry->is_cache_clean);
		existing = radix_tree_lookup(&dma_active_cacheline, cln);
```
It inserts `entry` if it's the first. If it's an overlap (`-EEXIST`), it increments the overlap count of the EXISTING cacheline entry in the radix tree, and does NOT insert the new `entry` into the radix tree.
The new `entry` itself is stored in the bucket hash table!
When unmapping, it removes from the bucket hash table, and calls `active_cacheline_remove`, which decrements the overlap count. If the overlap count reaches 0, it removes it from the radix tree.
So tracking is correctly balanced regardless of the warning.
There is no memory leak.

You are a concurrency expert reviewing Linux kernel locking mechanisms.     "thought": "**Analyzing a DMA Debugging Patch**

Okay, so I'm looking at this patch that tweaks `kernel/dma/debug.c`, specifically the `add_dma_entry()` function. It seems they've added a condition, `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`, within an `else if` branch that handles the `-EEXIST` error code. Let's dig into the context of this.

Right, the code block in question: it's about checking for overlapping DMA mappings. If `active_cacheline_insert` returns `-EEXIST`, it means there's a conflict. The original code already has a few checks in place (like `DMA_ATTR_SKIP_CPU_SYNC` and `is_cache_clean`). The added condition looks like a finer-grained check related to cache line alignment.

My first thought is always to check the definition of `dma_get_cache_alignment()`.  Ah, it's defined as `cache_line_size()` on some architectures.  And on ARM64, `cache_line_size()` is just an integer returning function, which is fine.

Now, I'm thinking about locking.  `active_cacheline_insert` is called *before* this condition. I don't see any obvious locks held during the execution of this `if` statement. But, wait, `add_dma_entry` does use a spinlock with the `get_hash_bucket` and `put_hash_bucket` pair, acquiring and releasing a spinlock and saving/restoring flags. And then it calls `active_cacheline_insert`, so the lock *should* be dropped before `dma_get_cache_alignment()` is called.

The `err_printk()` macro/function is used to print an error, so this added code seems to be suppressing a warning under certain circumstances.  It looks like in `dma/debug.c`, `err_printk` is just a function.

The core of the patch is really just this condition: `dma_get_cache_alignment() >= L1_CACHE_BYTES`. Is it safe to call this? Yes, it looks like it's just accessing a global or a constant or reading a CPU register, so no big issues there.  No sleep-in-atomic problems here.

Lock ordering? Nope, no locks are *added*. RCU issues? Not here.

Basically, this patch is straightforward. It looks like it's just suppressing a warning if the cache line alignment is smaller than the L1 cache line size.

Let me just quickly verify `dma_get_cache_alignment` in `include/linux/dma-mapping.h`...just to make sure. Okay, checked it. It's safe. Patch looks good.

Okay, so I'm looking at this patch that modifies `kernel/dma/debug.c`, specifically aiming to silence the `EEXIST` warning within `add_dma_entry()`. My initial thought is that I understand the intent: the author is trying to eliminate false positives on architectures like x86, where the hardware is cache-coherent, and thus overlapping DMA mappings within a cacheline aren't inherently problematic. The primary function of DMA-debug is to highlight cross-architecture portability issues—specifically, DMA mappings that might break on non-coherent architectures (like ARM32 or MIPS).

The core of my concern here is that by selectively suppressing the warning based on the current architecture's cache coherency (or, at least, a proxy for it), we're losing the ability to find a critical class of bugs. DMA-debug, running on x86, should ideally flag instances where a driver, even if functional on x86, is mapping data in a way that would lead to data corruption or other DMA problems on those other platforms. The patch does exactly the opposite; it's designed to ignore such conditions.

The existing code checks for overlapping DMA mappings. It will print the `EEXIST` error if, and only if, the DMA cache alignment is greater than or equal to `L1_CACHE_BYTES`. On x86, since `dma_get_cache_alignment()` returns 1, this condition is *never* met. Therefore, the warning is suppressed. This is where it falls apart, I think. This means the patch effectively disables the whole `EEXIST` check on x86, which is where a large chunk of development and testing occurs.

I get the concern about the false positives on x86 caused by adjacent kmalloc allocations which share a cacheline, but if it's already suppressed on ARM64 when SWIOTLB is active, the warning is already dead on ARM64 as well. Silencing the warning for every DMA mapping on x86, however, breaks the whole concept of cross-architecture DMA validation, the primary selling point of the DMA debug subsystem. If a driver maps two parts of the same struct that share a cacheline, the DMA debug tool running on x86 used to warn about it, and now it won't. If this becomes widespread, x86 developers will no longer catch this class of portability bugs.

The commit message itself acknowledges this architectural difference, explicitly stating the goal of suppressing the warning on x86 because the hardware is cache-coherent. However, that misses the point of using x86 to test for portability issues! We want to *find* the places where the driver makes an assumption that is true on x86, but not true on other architectures.

There is a check in the code for `CONFIG_DMA_BOUNCE_UNALIGNED_KMALLOC`. When that is enabled, it bounces the memory around. Then, `dma-debug` suppresses the warning. The goal for bounce buffers on ARM64 is to deal with misalignment issues. But the patch ignores ALL non-aligned mappings on x86. I see the intention of the commit author, but the cure is worse than the disease.

A better solution would be to distinguish in DMA-debug between those cases where the sharing of a cacheline is okay (such as two different `kmalloc(8)` calls which happen to be adjacent), and the cases where the overlap reflects a more fundamental problem in the driver, such as mapping two fields of the same struct or a page mapping.

The consequence here is that, without the warning on x86, we're effectively losing the ability to catch a class of portability bugs, which is a major issue. My recommendation would be to revert this change and seek a different solution, such as differentiating the types of allocations.
